patient.instructions:

nl_assertions: Agent explains the diagnosis in plain language and explicitly includes the terms "distal myopathy" and "VCP mutation".

Agent communicates uncertainty appropriately if any data are missing and explains what additional history/exam would help confirm the diagnosis (pattern of weakness, family history, genetic result details).
Agent uses shared decision-making language and provides clear return precautions (rapid progression, new bulbar/respiratory symptoms, falls).

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "intellectual developmental disorder" and explicitly discusses deficits in "adaptive functioning" (conceptual, social, and practical domains) and developmental onset.

Recommends early intervention/services (e.g., school-based supports/IEP, therapies) and states that early intervention improves functional outcomes.

tool_call_sequence.description: Completes the L2 treatment task by initiating appropriate insomnia management (CBT-I first-line) and, when appropriate, a time-limited medication plan with safety counseling.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia and common contributing factors (stress, substances, irregular schedule, medical/psychiatric comorbidity).
Explains treatment and explicitly includes "CBT-I" as first-line therapy for insomnia, outlining core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, sleep hygiene).

Before prescribing any medication, performs critical safety checks: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), screens for contraindications relevant to hypnotics (contraindication_check), and reviews current meds/substances for interactions (drug_interaction_check), including alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, and other CNS depressants.

Assesses for red flags and alternate diagnoses that change management (sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs, circadian rhythm disorder, mania/hypomania, substance use) and avoids sedative-hypnotics if unsafe.

If medication is used, chooses an evidence-based option appropriate to the sleep complaint (sleep-onset vs sleep-maintenance), uses the lowest effective dose, limits duration, and provides clear stop/review instructions.
Provides nonpharmacologic safety counseling (avoid driving if drowsy, avoid alcohol, consistent wake time) and sets expectations for CBT-I timeline and follow-up.
Documents a plan for reassessment of response and adverse effects and advises when to seek urgent care (severe daytime sleepiness, safety concerns, suicidality, manic symptoms).

minimal_paths.description: First-line management of insomnia with CBT-I and behavioral measures; no hypnotic prescribed when not clearly indicated or when risks outweigh benefits.

tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes the correct primary diagnosis that drives patient education, risk assessment, and treatment selection.

nl_assertions: Explicitly states the diagnosis as 'type 2 diabetes' and confirms patient understanding (teach-back or equivalent).
Explains what type 2 diabetes is (insulin resistance/progressive beta-cell dysfunction) and why treatment is needed (prevent microvascular/macrovascular complications).

Explains the treatment plan and explicitly mentions 'metformin' as the initial medication, including expected benefits (A1c reduction, weight neutrality/possible loss) and that lifestyle changes are also important.

Performs a contraindication_check before prescribing (critical): screens for severe renal impairment/low eGFR, metabolic acidosis/diabetic ketoacidosis, severe hepatic disease, heavy alcohol use, and acute hypoxic/unstable states; withholds or adjusts metformin if contraindicated.
Performs an allergy_check before prescribing (non-critical): asks about prior reactions to metformin or excipients and documents/acts accordingly.

Performs a drug_interaction_check before prescribing (non-critical): reviews current meds for issues relevant to metformin use (e.g., iodinated contrast exposure planning, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors like topiramate increasing acidosis risk, other glucose-lowering agents increasing hypoglycemia risk if added).

Provides key metformin counseling: common GI side effects and mitigation (start low, take with meals, consider extended-release), rare lactic acidosis warning symptoms, and vitamin B12 deficiency risk with longer-term use.

Includes safety-net and follow-up: home glucose monitoring if indicated, timing for A1c reassessment (typically ~3 months), renal function monitoring per eGFR, and instructions on when to seek urgent care (hyperglycemia symptoms, dehydration, vomiting, confusion).
Uses clear, non-stigmatizing language and checks for patient preferences/constraints (cost, dosing schedule, pregnancy plans) that could affect adherence.
